_meta.description: Contextual glosses for 2 Nephi Isaiah chapters. Each entry keys on book:chapter:verse and provides phrase-level annotations with short, factual, non-interpretive notes to help readers understand historical and cultural references.
_meta.format: Each gloss has: phrase (the exact text to underline), note (the tooltip text, max ~15 words), and optionally category (geographic, cultural, literary, historical)

12:1.note: Not the prophet Amos; Amoz was Isaiah's father, likely of a noble Judahite family.

12:2.phrase: the mountain of the Lord's house
12:2.note: Mount Moriah in Jerusalem, site of Solomon's temple.

12:2.phrase: in the top of the mountains
12:2.note: Isaiah pictures the temple mount raised above all peaks — the highest point on earth.

12:3.phrase: house of the God of Jacob
out of Zion shall go forth the law
12:3.note: Zion and Jerusalem are used as parallel terms for the same holy city.

12:4.note: Farm tools forged from weapons — a reversal of Joel 3:10, which says the opposite.
Curved blades used to trim grapevines — a common tool in Israelite agriculture.

12:5.note: The covenant people of Israel, descended from the patriarch Jacob.

12:6.note: Filled with pagan practices from Mesopotamia — Babylon, Assyria, and surrounding cultures.
Fortune-tellers. The Philistines, Israel's coastal neighbors, were known for divination.

12:6.phrase: please themselves in the children of strangers
12:6.note: Forming alliances (or intermarrying) with foreign nations instead of trusting God.

12:7.note: Horses and chariots were offensive military technology — and a sign of reliance on human power rather than God (see Deut. 17:16).

12:9.note: "Mean" here means common or ordinary — the everyday person, not someone cruel.

12:12.phrase: the day of the Lord of Hosts
12:12.note: A recurring Old Testament concept: a future time when God acts decisively to judge the proud and deliver the humble.

12:13.note: The tallest, most prized trees in the ancient world — used to build palaces and temples. A symbol of towering pride.
Bashan (modern Golan Heights) was famous for its massive oak forests. Second only to Lebanon's cedars as a symbol of strength.

12:15.note: A fortified wall — the military defenses cities relied on for survival.

12:16.note: Large merchant vessels for long-distance trade. Tarshish was likely in Spain or Sardinia — the edge of the known world.
Probably luxury goods or fine craftsmanship — the Hebrew suggests beautiful display vessels or carved imagery.

13:1.note: Everything a society leans on — all its supports. Isaiah then lists what those supports are.

13:1.phrase: the whole staff of bread, and the whole stay of water
13:1.note: The most basic necessities — food and water supply. When these fail, everything collapses.

13:2.note: Someone skilled in reading omens or interpreting signs — not necessarily a positive role.
The elder — a community leader whose authority came from age and experience.

13:3.note: A military officer commanding a unit of fifty soldiers — a mid-rank leader in the Israelite army.
A man of social rank or influence — literally, one whose face is "lifted up" in the community.
A skilled craftsman — metalworker, builder, or engineer. "Cunning" here means expert, not deceitful.
One skilled in persuasive speech. Some scholars read the Hebrew as "skilled in charms" (enchanter).

13:4.phrase: children unto them to be their princes
13:4.note: Inexperienced, unqualified rulers — the opposite of the wise leaders just listed. A sign of total social collapse.

13:5.note: Social order inverted — the lowly disrespect those of rank. Isaiah is describing anarchy.

13:6.phrase: Thou hast clothing, be thou our ruler
13:6.note: Things are so desperate that simply owning a coat qualifies someone to lead — everyone else has been stripped bare.

13:9.phrase: their sin to be even as Sodom
13:9.note: Sodom (Genesis 18-19) was the ultimate example of a city destroyed for its wickedness. The comparison means Judah's sin is open and shameless.

13:12.phrase: children are their oppressors, and women rule over them
13:12.note: Not a statement about women or children in general — Isaiah is saying the expected leaders have failed, and the social order is upside down.

13:14.phrase: ye have eaten up the vineyard
13:14.note: The vineyard is Israel (see Isaiah 5:1-7). The leaders have devoured what they were supposed to tend.

13:15.phrase: grind the faces of the poor
13:15.note: A vivid image of economic oppression — crushing people like grain between millstones.

13:16.note: The wealthy women of Jerusalem. Isaiah uses their luxury as a symbol of the whole city's misplaced priorities.
A haughty posture — head tilted back, looking down at others.
Flirtatious glances. The Hebrew suggests eyes painted or decorated to attract attention.
Taking small, affected steps — possibly because of ankle chains that restricted their stride (see v. 18).

13:16.phrase: making a tinkling with their feet
13:16.note: Ankle bracelets with bells or ornamental chains that jingled with each step.

13:17.phrase: smite with a scab the crown of the head
13:17.note: A skin disease on the most visible part of the body — public humiliation replacing public vanity.

13:18.note: Ankle bracelets, probably with small bells or chains.
Ornamental hairnets or headbands, often beaded.
Crescent-shaped pendants worn as necklaces — "tires" means headdresses or ornaments, not wheels.

13:19.note: Veils or long scarves covering the face and neck.

13:20.note: Decorative ankle chains connecting the feet — these also caused the "mincing" walk of v. 16.
Perfume boxes or scent bottles — the Hebrew literally means "houses of the soul" (i.e., containers for fragrance).

13:22.note: Festival robes — fine garments kept for special occasions.
Outer cloaks or shawls draped over the body.
Despite the name, probably money-bags or ornamental purses — not hair pins.

13:23.note: Polished metal mirrors — not eyeglasses, which wouldn't exist for another two thousand years.

13:24.phrase: instead of a girdle, a rent
13:24.note: Instead of a fine sash, a rope. "Rent" here means a torn strip of cloth.
A richly embroidered chest garment — replaced by rough sackcloth, the garment of mourning.
A brand-mark (from captivity or slavery) replacing their carefully maintained appearance.

13:26.phrase: her gates shall lament and mourn
13:26.note: City gates were the center of public life — where courts met, business happened, and news spread. Empty gates meant a dead city.

13:26.phrase: she shall be desolate, and shall sit upon the ground
13:26.note: Sitting on the ground was the posture of mourning and humiliation (see Lamentations 1:1).

14:1.phrase: seven women shall take hold of one man
14:1.note: So many men have died in war (13:25) that seven women share one husband. The number seven suggests completeness — the devastation is total.

14:1.phrase: we will eat our own bread, and wear our own apparel
14:1.note: Normally a husband provided food and clothing. These women waive that right — they only want his name to avoid the social shame of being unmarried and childless.
In ancient Israel, being unmarried and childless was considered a deep disgrace for a woman.

14:2.note: A sprouting shoot from a seemingly dead stump — new life after devastation. A recurring prophetic image (see also Isa. 11:1, Jer. 23:5, Zech. 3:8).

14:2.phrase: them that are escaped of Israel
14:2.note: The surviving remnant — those who endure the judgment described in chapters 12-13.

14:3.phrase: written among the living in Jerusalem
14:3.note: An ancient concept of a divine registry — a book of those counted as belonging to God's people (see Exod. 32:32, Ps. 69:28).

14:4.phrase: the spirit of judgment and by the spirit of burning
14:4.note: Purification through judgment — like refining metal with fire to burn away impurities.

14:5.phrase: a cloud and smoke by day and the shining of a flaming fire by night
14:5.note: Echoes the Exodus (Exod. 13:21-22), when God guided Israel with a pillar of cloud by day and fire by night. The protection returns.

14:5.phrase: upon all the glory of Zion shall be a defence
14:5.note: The Hebrew word is chuppah — a canopy or wedding covering. God shelters the renewed Zion like a bride.

14:6.note: A shelter or booth — echoing both the wilderness tabernacle and the harvest booths of the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot).
